text: If you want to become an AI engineer in
2026, here is the exact six-month road
map that will get you there. And I know
what you're thinking. 6 months sounds
way too short, right? Don't you need a
lot of years of experience, a PhD? Don't
you need reading a lot of research
papers every day and somehow keeping up
with everything that's happening on
LinkedIn? No, you really don't. Here is
why. An AI engineer builds applications
powered by AI, which is primarily large
language models. We are not training
models from scratch. We're not doing
research. We are taking powerful AI
capabilities that already exist and
turning them into products that solve
real world problems. And that yes, you
can learn that in 6 months if you're
focused. Now, if you're watching this
video, you're probably feeling two
things at once. On one hand, you're
excited. AI engineering sounds
incredible. Every week, there is
something new. Whether it's a new model,
new tools, new startups, it feels like
the most exciting time to be in this
field. But on the other hand, you're
probably overwhelmed, maybe even
intimidated. Everyone else seems to
already know what they're doing, and
you're thinking, "How am I going to ever
keep up with this?" Let me tell you that
feeling is normal, but it is not
accurate. And in this video, I'll walk
you through exactly how I would approach
this starting from scratch, month by
month, with real project and very honest
advice. If you don't know me, I'm Ashwar
Shawasan. I've been in the ML and AI
space for over 10 years. I got my mers
in data science from Columbia University
and I've worked at companies like
Microsoft, Google and IBM. Currently I
lead AI developer relations at Fireworks
AI which is one of the top AI startups
out there. So I've seen what actually
gets people hired whether it's in big
tech or fastmoving startups. I'll share
all of my favorite resources at the end
so definitely stick around for that. Now
the question is who is this road map
for? The most common path into AI
engineering is definitely software
engineering. If you've been working as a
backend or a full stack developer,
you've already know how to build
systems, you know how to work with APIs,
and you know how to ship to production.
Adding AI on top of that is pretty
natural. But that's not the only way to
get it. I have seen front-end developers
also make this transition. If you're
building user interfaces, you understand
how to create great experience. And AI
engineering is increasingly about AI
powered interfaces, whether it's chat
bots, co-pilots, or assistants. I've
seen product managers switch into AI
engineering as well. If you deeply
understand user problems and you're
willing to get technical, you have a
massive advantage. What I have seen
mattered in most case wasn't their
previous title. It was the willingness
to put in the effort that you need. Now,
here is the good news. You don't have to
be an expert coder to start. You need to
be comfortable enough with Python to
call APIs, to process data, and build
basic applications. That is a much lower
bar than most people think. Now, if that
sounds exciting, let's build your road
map. So, starting with month zero. I
call this month zero because this is the
part everybody rushes through or skips.
Then a few months later, they're making
viewer decisions because they never
understood the landscape that they're
operating in. So month zero is about
building your mental map of the entire
ecosystem. By the end of this month, you
should be able to look at any AI product
and understand roughly how it works,
what stack it is using, and what
trade-offs the builders made. So let me
break down that stack for you. At the
foundation, we have the models. These
come in two flavors. proprietary models
through APIs like OpenAI and Anthropic
and open-source models that you can
either run yourself or access through
inference providers like Fireworks AI or
Grock. Above that we have the retrieval
layer. This is how you give AR
applications access to specific
knowledge, your company's documents, the
product information, whatever you want
to put in. This involves embeddings,
vector databases and search. Then we
have the orchestration layer. So
frameworks like LANChain or Llama index
that helps application logic and tie
everything together. Finally, the
application layer which is the actual
interface that users interact with the
APIs that you expose, the integrations
that you build. So what you should
actually do this month is get your hands
dirty with different models. Sign up for
OpenAI, sign up for Anthropic and at
least one open-source provider. Try
making some API calls to these large
language models. Compare the responses
and then notice how different models
have different personalities, how they
have different strengths and how
different failure modes. This direct
experience is invaluable. Then I would
say you need to explore the tooling
landscape. You don't have to commit to
any particular framework yet. Just
explore. Look at Langchain, Llama Index
and the Open AI SDK. Read the docs,
understand what problems each tool is
solving, and then study how existing AI
products work. Pick five products that
you admire. let's say chat GPD,
perplexity, cursor and whatever. Then
reverse engineer them. What is probably
happening behind the scenes and then
make decisions about where to start.
Pick one model provider to learn deeply.
Pick one framework to build with. You
can expand on that later, but starting
focused gets you further faster. Then in
month one is where you go from
understanding the landscape to actually
working with models efficiently. And I
need you to hear this. Prompting is not
a soft skill. It is engineering. The
difference between a prompt that kind of
works and the one that works reliably in
production is huge. And that difference
comes down to systemic thinking. So
first understand how these models work
conceptually. You don't need transformer
architecture details but understand
tokenization, how text gets broken into
pieces and why it matters for cost and
context limits. Understand context
windows, how much information a model
can consider and what happens when you
exceed it. Understand things like
temperature and other parameters and how
they affect randomness and creativity of
a model. Second, learn to write prompts
that actually work. Clear instructions
matter. So instead of saying just
summarize this, try something like
summarize this document into three
bullet points focusing on key decisions
and their rational. Learn few short
prompting giving examples of what you
want. This is incredibly powerful for
consistent outputs. Then learn
structured outputs. Getting clean JSON
from a LLM sounds simple but it is
tricky. So learn response formats,
output validation, and how to handle it
when the model ignores your
instructions. Third, understand the
practical stuff. Streaming responses so
that users don't have to wait for the
complete generation of the answer. And
then things like error handling when the
APIs fail, rate limits and retry logic,
cost management, etc. Because every
token costs money. Now in this month, I
want you to build three small
applications. A domain specific
assistant. So pick a topic that you know
well. Build something that answers
questions knowledgeably. This teaches
you system prompts and context setting.
Then build a structured data extractor.
So start with messy text and extract
structured information from it. This
teaches you output formatting and
validation. Then build a multi-turn
conversational app, something where
previous messages matter. This teaches
you conversation history and context
management. And throughout all of this,
develop an iteration workflow. When a
prompt fails, don't randomly tweak it.
Analyze what went wrong. Form a
hypothesis, make a targeted change, and
test it again. This systematic approach
separates consistent results from the
lucky ones. Now month two is where you
go from experimentation to real
application building. The key shift here
is you start thinking in systems and not
just individual prompts. Real
applications have multiple components
working together. Whether it is handling
input, whether it's processing, calling
multiple prompts or models, whether it's
handling errors, whether it's presenting
results, the most important concept
you'll learn in this month is tool use,
also known as function calling. Here is
the thing about LLMs on their own. They
can only generate text. They can't check
calendars. They can't send you emails.
They can't look up information or they
can't even run code. Tool use is how you
give them those capabilities. You define
functions that the models can call. It
decides when to use them and with what
parameters. Your code executes them and
then returns the value. This is
fundamental. Almost every useful
applications involves the AI actually
doing something not just talking. So now
in this particular month I want you to
build a tool using assistant that takes
real actions. It could be doing things
like searching the web, reading files,
calling an API that you care about. And
the key here is doing real things, not
just chatting with the user. This needs
to be an application with multiple
steps, something called as chaining. So
build an application where one LLM
informs the next. It could be a research
assistant that searches, synthesizes
findings, then formats everything for
the user or something that handles
different input types whether it is
text, images, if you're using multimodal
model, it could be files and this
flexibility teaches you what real
applications really require. So I would
say pick one framework and learn it
well. Langchain is popular with tons of
documentation. Llama Index is great for
document processing or just build more
from scratch with SDKs and understanding
what frameworks do under the hood really
matters regardless of what you use. Here
is what you'll learn the hard way this
month. Token costs add up fast. A prompt
using 2,000 tokens when 500 would work
is four times more cost at scale and
latency matters more than you think.
Users are impatient. A 10-second
response might be correct but
practically unusable. And prompts that
work 90% of the time are not production
ready. That 10% failure will drive users
crazy. So think about these edge cases
from the very beginning. Then in month
three, pay really close attention here.
A huge chunk of real world AI
engineering is RAG, which is retrieval
augmented generation. This is how you
give AI applications access to specific
knowledge. Whether it's company
documents, product databases, or
internal Wikipedia pages. I'm not
talking about flashy demos. I'm talking
about reliable systems that work
consistently over time. So, here is what
you need to know. Starting with
chunking, how you split documents
matters more than people realize.
Whether it should be paragraphs,
sentences, fixed token count, should you
preserve the structure, there is no
single right answer, but your choice
dramatically affects the retrieval
quality. Then we have embeddings. This
is how text becomes numbers that capture
meaning. Different embedding models have
different strengths. Some handle
semantic similarity better while the
others handle long passages better. Then
we have vector databases where you store
embeddings and search them. Popular
vector databases are Pine Cone, Vivviet,
Chroma and Qrand. Now each of them have
different trade-offs around scale, cost
and features. Then you have retrieval
strategies. Simple similarity search is
just the start. You also have hybrid
search which combines semantic and
keyword matching. Then you should learn
about reanking which uses a second model
to improve the results of retrieval.
Then you also have query transformation
which rewrites the questions to get
better retrieval. Here is the critical
insight that you need to know. Most drag
systems fail because retrieval is weak
not because the LLM is bad. You feed the
model with irrelevant context and the
output suffers no matter how smart the
model is. So in this particular month I
want you to build one serious rag
application end to end. Use data that
you can find publicly whether it's open
source documentations, papers, articles
etc. and then build the complete
pipeline in chunking embedding storage
retrieval and generation and then build
an interface where people can actually
go and use it. Here is what will set you
apart. Evaluation. Most people have no
idea if their rag system actually works
well. Even basic evaluation puts you
ahead. So create a test set with
questions where you know the answers and
which documents should be retrieved.
Measure whether you're retrieving the
right documents or not. Measure the
answer accuracy and track these metrics
as you make changes. This evaluation
mindset is incredibly valuable and
surprisingly rare. Then month four is
where things get very interesting
because we have agents. Agents are AI
systems that take multiple steps to
accomplish goals, which is deciding what
actions to take along the way. Instead
of just responding to a single query, an
agent might search for an information,
analyze what it found, realize that it
might need more, so search again, and
then synthesize everything, and then
finally respond to the user. This is
where AI engineering starts to feel like
building genuinely intelligent systems.
So this is where you need to understand
the core agent loop. The observe state,
decide on an action, execute, observe
the result and repeat until done. Then
secondly, understand planning. How do
agents break down complex tasks? Here we
have different approaches. One is chain
of thought, then the other is tree of
thought along with other planning
frameworks. And third, understand
memory. Short-term memory within a task,
long-term memory across sections. How do
you actually implement these? Then
fourth thing, understand error handling.
Agents do fail a lot. APIs often error
out. Tools return unexpected results.
Agents go down wrong paths. So you need
to understand all of this. So this
particular month, what I want you to
build is build an AI agent that
accomplishes a real multi-step task.
Maybe a research agent that takes a
question, searches through multiple
sources, synthesizes what it found, and
produces a final result. making
decisions about what to search based on
what it's discovering. Or you can build
a coding agent that reads a code
database, understands the functions,
makes the changes, and verifies the
work. Or you can even build a data
analysis agent that examines a data set,
decides what to ask, and runs analysis,
and then finally presents the finding.
The specific domain matters less than
building something that genuinely
reasons through multiple steps. Through
this process, you'll learn that agents
are expensive. Each step involves LLM
calls. 10 step might cost 10x what a
single turn application costs. Then you
learn that agents are slow. Multiple
round trips actually add up on time. So
think about user experience. Streaming
progress and showing what's happening.
Then agents are unpredictable with the
same input, different parts. Testing is
hard. You need observability. You need
logging what the agent does and why.
Simpler agents are usually better. So
before building a complex agent, ask
yourself if a simple chain or even a
single prompt could work. Then in month
five, we're going to focus on deployment
and production. This is going to be your
biggest edge because this is where most
people stop learning. So projects
running only on your laptop create zero
business value. A deployed system that
real users can use and is running
reliably is what is very important. So I
would highly recommend you learn fast
API for building APIs. It is the
standard for AI deployments. Then I
would say learn Docker for
containerization. Your app that runs
everywhere is what is reliable. Then
learn basic cloud deployment. You can
use tools like railway render or fly IO.
It makes it easy without having DevOp
experience. Then take your rag system or
agent from the previous months and just
deploy it. You can add logging and
observability. For example, what
requests are coming in? what's the LLM
generating? Can you debug when the
things break? So use tools like
Langsmith, Langfuse or even just
structured looping which gives you
visibility. Then I would say get into
latency monitoring. How long are
requests taking? Where are the
bottlenecks? Then I would say get into
cost tracking. How much are you paying
per request? How much are you paying per
day? How much are you paying per user?
Then look into error handling. What
happens when LLM APIs time out? When do
rate limits hit? When do models return
garbage? Handle these things gracefully.
Then look into caching. If someone asks
the same question twice, maybe skip the
LLM call entirely. This dramatically
reduces cost and latency. So here are
some practical tips. Start with the
simplest deployment that works. Use
environment variables for API keys and
never hardcode your secrets. Always set
up for health checks and always set up
for basic alerting. This production
mindset is what hiring managers mean by
experience. It is not about the number
of years you've worked at a company. It
is about your experience in building
production level systems. Then in month
six, it is time to specialize and
prepare for your interviews. I would say
at this point, you need to stop being a
generalist. Look at real AI engineer job
descriptions. What are the skills that
keep appearing? What are the tools that
keep appearing? What kind of projects
are they requiring from you? Pick a
direction based on your interest.
Whether it's userfacing AI products
which focuses on application development
and great user experience or if it's
infrastructure which involves deployment
scaling ML ops for LLM applications or
if it's agents and autonomous systems
which has complex architectures and
cuttingedge patterns or it could be in
open-source models where you're
fine-tuning using Laura quantization and
optimization. I would say build one
capstone project you can walk through
confidently in these interviews. You can
also combine multiple skills. You can
combine rag with agents deployment and
evaluation. Prepare your explanations
properly. Interviewers want to
understand how you think, why this
problem, why this approach, what
trade-offs did you make, what broke and
how did you fix it, what would you do
differently. And the other thing I
highly recommend people to do is build
in public. If you are building portfolio
projects, share that on LinkedIn,
Twitter, GitHub, Substack, wherever
you're comfortable. Write about what
you're learning and visibility
definitely creates opportunities. So you
need to do that. So let me leave you
with this. AI engineering is genuinely
new and the playbook is still being
written. That is scary, but it is also
exciting. The barriers to entry are low
than traditional engineering. You don't
need a CS degree. You just need to build
things that work. This field rewards
builders over studers. You can watch
tutorials forever, but none of it would
matter if you are not building. Every
project teaches you things that reading
never will. Resources are in the
